item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The only thing I like about this, and i like it a lot, is the fact that WE HAVE NO IDEA WHAT IS IN THAT HOUSE!!!!!

Seriously. There could be an unkillable lizard in there, or a literal army of the Church of the Broken God.

We. Will. NEVER. Know.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Does the effect stop when you leave the building? Lots of details don't make sense otherwise, but I'd think the researchers would say so directly…

item.title: Critique
item.content:encoded:
Before starting, I'd like to state that I'm kind of new to the site and thus my criticism should be viewed as from someone new to this site's standards.

+ show block
– hide block

"Several city blocks around SCP-2329 have been purchased by the Foundation and a security system (consisting of multiple points of surveillance and neutralization of intruders) should be maintained in this perimeter."

I think saying "should be maintained" implies that it's up for debate as to whether or a security system should be in place, I'd suggest removing the "should" and making it more definite.

"It has been kept up to code with all the necessary functionality required by local government since that point but has not been altered in any other way."

I think it's a little unnecessary to state that it's kept up to date with the "local government's requirements" and doesn't really describe the SCP, it would fit moreso into Containment procedures and even then isn't truly necessary (a grammatical error is present as well, " functionality required by local government", it should be the local government, as you're speaking of a specific object).

"On both a structural and a visual level SCP-2329 closely resembles the buildings surrounding it and individuals are able to enter and exit it on their own free will."

The sentence just feels a little off to me, as the document previously states that the apartment building is under lock-down when not used for testing, it would be better if you stated that the building is accessible.

"This area of effect also extends to inanimate objects possessed by another individual (e.g. clothes, hand-carried objects); if an individual drops an object they were carrying it will become perceivable by all other individuals, although it will not be perceived to have suddenly appeared but rather that the observer simply had not paid attention to it before. It additionally extends to all sensory input, not just sight – test subjects are unable to hear sounds caused by other subjects, cannot smell one another, and cannot feel one another even if forced into such close proximity that physical contact would be unavoidable. "

Here you state the effects of SCP-2329 on "all sensory input", especially with this sentence "It additionally extends to all sensory input, not just sight – test subjects are unable to hear sounds caused by other subjects, cannot smell one another, and cannot feel one another even if forced into such close proximity that physical contact would be unavoidable."

Yet you previously state " Some perception still exists at a subconscious level as individuals inside SCP-2329 will make an effort to avoid bumping into one another"

What does "perception at a subconscious level" mean? You've already estabilished that the SCP affects perception, preventing anyone from perceiving anyone else, yet you then state that slight perception is possible. Then you go on to state that the effects of the SCP extends to all sensory input.
If there is no sensory input or perception of others, how can subjects instinctively avoid each other? To do so would require them to somehow know about each other being nearby, which would indicate some form of perception is possible. It's a bit of a glaring contradiction regarding the effects of the SCP.


Please collapse long posts. ~Zyn

Otherwise, I think it was a very interesting read and an original concept, I quite enjoyed this SCP and hope to see more works from you. +1 from me

(Also, apologies for the long paragraph, I don't know how to collapse these)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For those unaware this has now been read by east side Steve SCP show on YouTube. Just thought I'd share since I ain't seen anyone here mention it yet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this. It reminds me a lot of The Twilight Zone but more brutal. Man complains of noise, tries to take things into his own hands, makes a minor (but clearly containable) tear in reality, dies, leaves it behind for everyone else to fall into. A spell gone wrong - fabulous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So…You can't physically touch anyone, yet some guy got trampled?

at a subconscious level…individuals inside SCP-2329 will make an effort to avoid bumping into one another

Oh ok, so they can touch each other but subconsciously avoid it.

cannot feel one another even if forced into such close proximity that physical contact would be unavoidable

Wait what? This could possibly be read as they simply don't notice that they're being touched, but it should be clarified. Right now it seems like the article contradicts itself and people subconsciously avoid possible contact, but if it's unavoidable they can just walk through each other, but they can still trample an old dude?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: how to escape the police 101

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How did I remain unaware of this?!?

I can imagine that it might be inconvenient to get a craftsman to do some work in one of the apartments. He can not see me, I can not see him. How can I tell him what to do? (Or even tell him that I need his service?)
Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry for necro, but if u are talking about the 4 dudes on the chairs, most likely it's because someone asked them to sit there?

item.title: Re: Photoplasty Link To SCP
item.content:encoded:
http://forums.cracked.com/viewtopic.php?f=25&t=1507&p=40677.
Some of the images have a link to this SCP.

item.title: Re: Photoplasty Link To SCP
item.content:encoded: Could you send a link to the photoplasty please?

item.title: Photoplasty Link To SCP
item.content:encoded: A cracked.com photoplasty contest had a link to here! I think it was culture if the internet vanished.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is creepy as fuck and I love it. Empty places are creepy, being alone is creepy, the trampling death is horrifying… but the idea that really gets me is that the Foundation deals with lots of dangerous people, and this is a perfect hiding place.

One question. The description says that people inside can't perceive other people who are inside, and that people outside can't perceive anyone who's inside. Experiment 16 (the one with the TV) seems to indicate that people inside also can't perceive anyone who is /outside/- they don't just perceive the building as empty apart from themselves, they perceive the /world/ as empty apart from themselves until they leave the building. Is this correct? If so, it should be noted in the description as part of the anomaly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Same here. My mind was definitely going toward the "why are you assuming those other people are actually there when there's no evidence to support that theory?" I'd say failing to address this would have been a flaw in the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

I liked the third experiment being in there. I'd been having the same thoughts. This distinguishes it from the extra-dimensional overlap pieces we have on site and brings the reader closer to the authors intent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Huh, I really should have gotten to this article sooner, as it is a good one.

I'm in agreement that the photo here really adds a lot of flavor to this article, especially the caption. Additionally, the test log provides some interesting ways for the foundation to narrow in on the anomaly being an absolute rule, and the final story adds just a dash of horror to round out the piece.

+1. It was a fun read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I seem to be missing the explanation as to how the staff of the Foundation can perceive people from inside the apartment. If that is lacking, I would think that would be a bit of a plot hole.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The basic idea is more or less identical to SCP-451, only temporary, multiple-targeted, and location based.

That would've turned me off (as this one has less story to tell than that 451's log), but the death of the tenant was so brutal and horrific it pulled me back in.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Of course, the response of 911 would be to dispatch police to investigate why they kept getting calls where there was no one on the line…

…and then the police show up, and the building is empty except for the body of this guy who's been trampled to death…

…my God, that's creepy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Initially, this reminded me of that hotel room that duplicates people, but on quick reflection, I had to wonder why, because they're nothing alike.

I like having Dr. Warm's hypothesis in there (I was wondering the same thing myself); the only thing I would change is the final line, explaining where the wounds came from. Let that be fridge horror, I think it'll work excellently. Otherwise, good work, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hell is (the absence of) other people.

This has a quiet creepiness that appeals to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Since the photo shows what is behind the people who are supposed to be in it, I'd guess you'd be able to see a sign through them. Unless you are only seeing what your brain expects to see and not what is actually there (sort of like the actual blind spot in your eye where the optic nerve enters, which your brain fills in depending on what is around it), then you might just see a blur of vaguely word-like shapes or blank wall if they are covering the entire thing. In that case, things like wood grain might also change depending on if someone is standing in front of it, since your mind would just be making something up to fill in the space (unless you are very familiar with it and know what it should look like).

(Author can certainly correct me, just my thoughts.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm thinking it's a little bit of a Schrodinger's Box effect, and partly exploiting the fact that there are mechanical limits to the non-observation effect. It doesn't directly detect observe people- only the weight of the box-o-unknown-contents it's attached to- and it doesn't report that information to anything but it's own cutoff switch. Basically it works for the same reason that the guy got trampled- the other people are still there as far as the laws of physics are concerned, you just can't directly perceive them or their actions.

Personally, I'd want to hook up a readout to the sensor, and see whether:

  • a) it would show you the actual weight of the elevator and contents1
  • b) it ceased to detect the other people and let the over-capacity elevator try to work2
  • or c) it showed you only your own weight but still functioned properly by not letting the elevator work when over capacity.
Footnotes
1. which would still tell you nothing definitive about the presence of other people, only that this was an exceptionally heavy elevator and that it may or may not change its weight randomly when you aren't looking.
2. because each person saw only their own weight, and none of them triggered the sensor individually.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It is currently unknown whether this is due to the anomalous properties of SCP-2329 causing all instrumentation to fail or if the instrumentation is working as expected but observers ignore all output that would indicate the existence of sentient life.

If either is the case, how come the lift "weight alarm" thingy (my words) triggered?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What would happen if someone obstructed something, like a sign? Would other occupants be able to read the sign if the other person was between them and it? I'd be interested to know. Cool and original idea though, still. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked Dr. Warm's theory - I felt like it heightens the feeling of uncertainty and strangeness around the issue. That being said, the specific way it's written, where it pops up as an aside in the test log, rather than being a primary focus of a sentence, is suboptimal. Something like "One proposed explanation for this phenomenom, due to Dr. Ward, held that subjects existed in separate universes. However, Test 22 disproves this theory because … and indicates that the phenomenon at work blocks perception."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The effect only extends to not noticing any living people and anything they're holding.

So the moment Mr Ziggman ceased to be a living person and instead a dead body, everyone could notice him. Although this implies every person in the building individually finds the corpse and would try to call 911, ending in a great number of calls to 911 with people complaining that they want to report a dead body.

Also the landlord would be unaware of the anomaly because I believe that Mr Ziggman caused it.
Complaints about noisy tennants, then suddenly none more and nobody in the building can notice another any longer… Except of course that as the originator of the anomaly, Mr Ziggman was probably only recipient of the 'not noticing' effect and not the 'instinctually avoid others' effect.

Sounds like a Monkey's Paw wish or a Deal with the Devil.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I knew I was forgetting something, edited my first post to include thanks to the people who helped me.

As for Dr. Warm's contention, let me try to explain what my thinking was there. The sole reason for its inclusion was to show how incredibly difficult it is to test a thing whose anomalous property is that it can't be observed; I went into way more detail on this in my first draft before smarter people than me noted that it really made the article drag, so I cut most of that stuff out. I wasn't trying to go full on SCP-055 (which does that whole thing better than I ever could) but I did at least want to hint at how tough it is to figure out creative ways to actually figure out the parameters of this anomaly.

Also, I had it in my mind that pretty much everyone at the Foundation immediately assumed it was memetic in nature and that Dr. Warm was just coming up with a fringe idea, which is why I used the word "contention" rather than "theory". The point I was making was that it took 22 separate tests before they could actually conclusively disprove it despite it being a fairly goofy idea. That's how hard it was to test.

If all that doesn't make sense then I'll remove the line. If it makes sense but doesn't come across in the article then I'll figure out a way to rewrite it so it's clearer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Agreed on the statement re: Dr. Warm (bit of a silly name, in my opinion, but I've seen goofier). Seems like it can be taken out without hurting the piece too much?

there's something eerie about that photo, and knowing that there's supposed to be 4 people in that picture makes it eerier

That's what I like the most about this article. That, and the ending note about the boots. There are plenty of deaths caused by tramplings out there (Black Friday sales, anyone?) but the fact that these people aren't stampeding, aren't even trying to push someone out of the way, but are doing so anyway, lends a strong lingering afterthought of "what am I failing to notice in my life?"

Anyway, I remember seeing this in the forums, and liking it there too. +1 from me.

Author, consider thanking the other people who helped you out in chat/PMs in your first post~

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Unless I'm missing something, how did they find the dead guy? The whole last addendum raises a lot of questions really. Otherwise, I can dig this. Neutral vote.

EDIT: I'm dumb, just got confused by the wording, specifically how the landlord "was" not aware of the anomaly, implying that it had already been in effect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dislike the inclusion of Dr. Warm's theory (there isn't any evidence to support Warm's hypothesis besides 'nobody can see anybody else' and Occam's razor indicates that a cognitohazardous or perceptive effect is the most likely scenario), but there's something eerie about that photo, and knowing that there's supposed to be 4 people in that picture makes it eerier.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Image is mildly photoshopped from: https://www.flickr.com/photos/north-carolina-state-archives/16568007397/

It has no copyright license associated with it and it's old enough that it's probably public domain but I figured I'd attribute it anyway, better safe than sorry.

EDIT: Belated thanks for feedback to DrMorris, Zyn, MrWrong, and a bunch of people in chat whose names I didn't think to note down and now feel guilty for forgetting.
